title: remote,build: ignore if `.containerignore` or `.dockerignore` is a symlink outside of buildContext
body:
Drop support for remote use-cases when `.containerignore` or `.dockerignore` is a symlink pointing to arbitrary location on host.

```release-note
remote,build: do not allow to process arbitrary symlinks on host for remote builds.
```

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *flouthoc* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [flouthoc] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

Can you just use 	securejoin "github.com/cyphar/filepath-securejoin"?

I think this will throw an error if .dockerignore or .containerignore point at a link outside of the root path. That way I could have a link within the context dir.

> I think this will throw an error if .dockerignore or .containerignore point at a link outside of the root path

@rhatdan I think SecureJoin does not throws error if link is outside the root path, it just resolves the symlink and concats with the `root`. So final path is created something as `/root/some/abs/resolved/symlink`. In such cases since path does not exists so no error is relyed to user it just silently ignores the `.containerignore` or `.dockerignore` file without notifying the user.

However it works correctly if symlink is inside the `root` or relative to `root`.

Following is the diff which i tried
```diff
diff --git a/pkg/bindings/images/build.go b/pkg/bindings/images/build.go
index aabc7290d..63d354ad1 100644
--- a/pkg/bindings/images/build.go
+++ b/pkg/bindings/images/build.go
@@ -27,6 +27,7 @@ import (
        "github.com/containers/storage/pkg/ioutils"
        "github.com/docker/go-units"
        "github.com/hashicorp/go-multierror"
+       securejoin "github.com/cyphar/filepath-securejoin"
        jsoniter "github.com/json-iterator/go"
        "github.com/sirupsen/logrus"
 )
@@ -754,16 +755,16 @@ func errIfSymlink(path string) error {
 }
 
 func parseDockerignore(root string) ([]string, error) {
-       path := filepath.Join(root, ".containerignore")
-       err := errIfSymlink(path)
+       path, err := securejoin.SecureJoin(root, ".containerignore")
+       //err := errIfSymlink(path)
        if err != nil {
                return nil, err
        }
        ignore, err := os.ReadFile(path)
        if err != nil {
                var dockerIgnoreErr error
-               path = filepath.Join(root, ".dockerignore")
-               symlinkErr := errIfSymlink(path)
+               path, symlinkErr := securejoin.SecureJoin(root, ".dockerignore")
+               //symlinkErr := errIfSymlink(path)
                if symlinkErr != nil {
                        return nil, symlinkErr
                }
```

> That way I could have a link within the context dir.

@rhatdan even though secure-join is not working as expected but I have modified function to `errIfSymlinkOutsideRoot` which should handle this use-case.

But wouldn't securejoin work ok since when you attempt to open the path, it would fail with file does not exist?

If I set .containerignore->/etc/shadow; don't you get an  error like
/ROOTDIR/etc/shadow does not exist

> But wouldn't securejoin work ok since when you attempt to open the path, it would fail with file does not exist?
> 
> If I set .containerignore->/etc/shadow; don't you get an error like /ROOTDIR/etc/shadow does not exist

@rhatdan I think It does not fails cause `parseDockerIgnore` ignores `IsNotExists` https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/bindings/images/build.go#L751, I think its because the ignore files are not direct input by users but detected by podman internally.

OK
LGTM
But tests are failing.
@rhatdan They were flakes CI is green now.
@containers/podman-maintainers PTAL

I tried out  f9ae0309e4213bc9094054bde0249c17bd681736
It's possible to detect that the directory _/etc/testdir_ exists on the host (that is outside of the context directory). 
The build fails when the directory does not exist. I don't know if this matters or not.

```
$ pwd
/home/test/srcdir
$ ls -la
total 8
drwxr-xr-x. 2 test test   62 Oct 28 07:31 .
drwx------. 8 test test 4096 Oct 28 07:24 ..

lrwxrwxrwx. 1 test test   42 Oct 28 07:22 .dockerignore -> /etc/testdir/../../proc/self/cwd/emptyfile
-rw-r--r--. 1 test test    0 Oct 28 07:21 emptyfile
$ cat Dockerfile
FROM scratch
$
```
#### case 1: The directory /etc/testdir exists

```
$ ls -ld /etc/testdir
drwxr-xr-x. 2 root root 6 Oct 28 07:32 /etc/testdir
$ pwd

$ podman --remote build --no-cache .
STEP 1/1: FROM scratch
COMMIT
--> 1ef5892c2c0

```

#### case 2: The directory /etc/testdir does not exist

```
$ ls -ld /etc/testdir
ls: cannot access '/etc/testdir': No such file or directory
$ pwd
/home/test/srcdir
$ podman --remote build --no-cache .
Error: unable to resolve symlink /home/test/srcdir/.dockerignore: lstat /etc/testdir: no such file or directory
$
```

#### about the Podman version
```
$ podman version
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.3.0-dev
API Version:  4.3.0-dev
Go Version:   go1.18.6
Git Commit:   f9ae0309e4213bc9094054bde0249c17bd681736
Built:        Fri Oct 28 07:52:08 2022
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```
@eriksjolund That was one of the reason to mask this error https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/16315#discussion_r1007240297 , I think we should mask this error.
@eriksjolund Try again plz.

> @eriksjolund Try again plz. 

@flouthoc I'm not able to do that right now but I could check during the weekend.  I'm also a bit curious to investigate if there is any way to combine github.com/cyphar/filepath-securejoin with some additional checks in parseDockerIgnore(). 

Another thing: I think it would be more secure to use the resolved path _resolvedPath_
```
resolvedPath, _ := filepath.EvalSymlinks(path)
```
when reading the file. The symlinks might have changed after the check (TOCTOU).

I think it would be good to aim for docker buildkit compatibility  (i.e. running `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build ...`)

It looks like docker resolves a _.dockerignore_ symlink as if the build context directory is the root of the file system.

```
$ docker --version
Docker version 20.10.17, build 100c701
$ pwd
/Users/test/dir
$ ls -la
total 16
drwxr-xr-x  7 test  test  224 Oct 28 20:04 .
drwxr-xr-x  3 test  test   96 Oct 28 19:42 ..
lrwxr-xr-x  1 test  test    7 Oct 28 20:04 .dockerignore -> /ignore
-rw-r--r--  1 test  test   41 Oct 28 19:42 Dockerfile
-rw-r--r--  1 test  test    0 Oct 28 19:42 a1
-rw-r--r--  1 test  test    0 Oct 28 19:42 a2
-rw-r--r--  1 test  test    3 Oct 28 20:03 ignore
$ cat Dockerfile
FROM docker.io/library/alpine
COPY / /dir
$ cat ignore
a1
$ DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build -q --no-cache -t test .
sha256:4eda78871f8fe0e92e4190fdbe27477b5e7e7cb74d860e8e3e721e38a24523eb
$ docker run --rm test ls /dir
Dockerfile
a2
ignore
$ ln -sf ../ignore .dockerignore
$ ls -la

drwxr-xr-x  7 test  test  224 Oct 28 20:04 .
drwxr-xr-x  3 test  test   96 Oct 28 19:42 ..
lrwxr-xr-x  1 test  test    7 Oct 28 20:14 .dockerignore -> ../ignore
-rw-r--r--  1 test  test   41 Oct 28 19:42 Dockerfile
-rw-r--r--  1 test  test    0 Oct 28 19:42 a1

sha256:9f77e227de43139bba6edcca4543909d39efca7bd410f641b2facc0655bf5a01
$ docker run --rm test ls /dir

a2

@flouthoc with your new commit 4b242b2 the error message became

```
Error: /home/test/srcdir/.dockerignore cannot be a symlink outside of build context
```

instead of the previous result from https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/16315#issuecomment-1294506978

```
Error: unable to resolve symlink /home/test/srcdir/.dockerignore: lstat /etc/testdir: no such file or directory
```

#### About using github.com/cyphar/filepath-securejoin   
from https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/16315#issuecomment-1293301950
> In such cases since path does not exists so no error is relyed to user it just silently ignores the .containerignore or .dockerignore file without notifying the user. 

One alternative could be to use filepath-securejoin  and at the same time introduce new  a command-line option for allowing _.dockerignore_ to be a symlink. 
Users using a symlink _.dockerignore_ would then be surprised by a failing build instead of a non-working _.dockerignore_  when they upgrade the Podman version.
After a few Podman releases, allowing symlink _.dockerignore_  could be made the default setting.

A side-note regarding Buildkit: At first sight it seems that filepath-securejoin behaves in the same way as Buildkit regarding following symlinks. That conclusion is just drawn from the two  Buildkit tests in
https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/16315#issuecomment-1295322849 
(In other words, the behaviour might differ when looking more closely)

#### About using  filepath.EvalSymlinks(path)

@flouthoc  4b242b2 (that is using `filepath.EvalSymlinks(path)`)  is fine by me. Paths outside of the build context directory may be traversed but I think that commit changes the current Podman functionality the least.
@eriksjolund I think securejoin approach is better if `buildkit` is just ignoring invalid symlinks instead of error, I'll amend this.

@flouthoc When I tested it out,  Builldkit ignored the invalid symlink
```
$ ls -la                                                        
total 8
drwxr-xr-x  4 test  test  128 Oct 29 10:21 .
drwxr-xr-x  3 test  test   96 Oct 28 19:42 ..
lrwxr-xr-x  1 test  test   11 Oct 29 10:21 .dockerignore -> nonexistent
-rw-r--r--  1 test  test   42 Oct 28 20:12 Dockerfile
$ DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build --no-cache -q .                
sha256:9a56b68578083e54c7883e0d8cf6b0eea2f1f7d724e17181fd07fc53ba845eb0
$ echo $?
0
$
```
@eriksjolund Following patch maintains parity with docker and buildkit, I have added a test to verify this.

@flouthoc I tried out ec21a3a and noticed that the server may receive a symlink .dockerignore. When symlinks are evaluated on the server side the resulting path is a file outside of the context directory. To achieve this I used an extra symlink 
```
$ ls -l d/d/d/d/d/d/
total 0
lrwxrwxrwx. 1 test test 17 Nov  2 19:17 d -> ../../../../../..
```

> @flouthoc I tried out [ec21a3a](https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/ec21a3a62874016e1b4cfcfac0ff7cf60eb8a566) and noticed that the server may receive a symlink .dockerignore. When symlinks are evaluated on the server side the resulting path is a file outside of the context directory. To achieve this I used an extra symlink
> 
> ```
> $ ls -l d/d/d/d/d/d/
> total 0
> lrwxrwxrwx. 1 test test 17 Nov  2 19:17 d -> ../../../../../..
> ```

@eriksjolund Should be handled now. Could you PTAL again.

I think a 54de6e7 client will not read outside of the context directory. 
A `podman system service` of 54de6e7 may read outside of the context directory if some other client (for example Podman 4.3.1) performs a remote build. (Just mentioning it as a side-note as this PR touches the client-side code)
@flouthoc There are other clients than Podman. (I haven't tested any other clients though).

> @eriksjolund Overall I'm not sure about about supporting every third party client, I think podman can only guarantee about `podman-remote`, `docker`, `API` and `compat API`. Do you think i missed any other client ?

I don't know so much about the different uses of `podman system service`.

> 
> Also if any third party client wants to process `symlink` and post it to podman via API it should still be allowed, cause they are using API directly, I don't think its a problem with `podman` but how a third party client has implemented it. But we can discuss on this.

That the server allows symlinks is fine but I think the server should not follow symlinks to the outside of the build context directory. I don't see how any client could depend on such functionality. (The result of the container build then starts to be dependent of the files and directories outside of the build context)

> 
> We can actually make it work for API as well if we move parts of symlink validation from `func parseDockerignore` to server end but not sure if that is expected thing to do ? ( It prohibits use-case where someone actually wants to send symlink in build context for API use-case but it is still support by regular podman and buildah )

Sharing code between client and server sounds like a good idea.
I agree we should be only resolving symlinks within the build context directory on the server.

> I agree we should be only resolving symlinks within the build context directory on the server.

@rhatdan @eriksjolund Done `.containerignore` validation happens at server end.

What is the expected behaviour when a `.dockerignore` is a symlink pointing to `../.dockerignore`? Should the symlink be followed? In the following test podman (fe14a5ee7e4c622ebad0acb17e8845427046a9a7) followed the symlink but not docker.

I could take another look over the weekend.

I wonder if podman (without --remote) needs to be adjusted as well? (See __test 3__)

@eriksjolund podman without remote is on the same host, user already has access to this file so I think users should be allowed use symlink when on the same host and this restriction should be only on `remote` use-cases. WDYT ?

@rhatdan WDYT ?

@flouthoc That's a fair point, that users already have access to the file, but I don't think users need the feature. It would be better if podman remote and non-remote behaviour would match. Another argument for not having the feature is that the docker behaviour (see  https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/16315#issuecomment-1333338785)  does not match the podman behaviour. 

The feature is actually available in docker if `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=0` and  the file outside of the source directory is not named _.dockerignore_ .

<details>
  <summary>Click me for a DOCKER_BUILDKIT=0 example</summary>

```
$ docker --version
Docker version 20.10.21, build baeda1f
$ ls -la                          
total 8
drwxr-xr-x  6 erik  wheel  192 Dec  4 09:25 .
drwxr-xr-x  4 erik  wheel  128 Dec  4 09:23 ..
lrwxr-xr-x  1 erik  wheel    7 Dec  4 09:25 .dockerignore -> ../file
-rw-r--r--  1 erik  wheel   42 Oct 23 13:46 Dockerfile
-rw-r--r--  1 erik  wheel    0 Dec  4 09:22 hello
-rw-r--r--  1 erik  wheel    0 Dec  4 09:22 world
$ cat Dockerfile 
FROM docker.io/library/alpine
COPY / /dir
$ cat .dockerignore 
hello
$ DOCKER_BUILDKIT=0 docker build --no-cache -q -t test .   
sha256:eebb326bb23e9f48e5530031208072f605eb9d2fb3466e9710c3b66ca107ba70
$ docker run --rm test ls -la /dir                      
total 12
drwxr-xr-x    2 root     root          4096 Dec  4 08:29 .
drwxr-xr-x    1 root     root          4096 Dec  4 08:29 ..
lrwxrwxrwx    1 root     root             7 Dec  4 08:25 .dockerignore -> ../file
-rw-r--r--    1 root     root            42 Oct 23 11:46 Dockerfile
-rw-r--r--    1 root     root             0 Dec  4 08:22 world
$
```
</details>


 The docker project sees this feature as a bug, though. The feature is not available when using BuildKit (i.e., using `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1`).

Yesterday I tried out local (i.e. non-remote) Podman and got different results when using either
the filename

_.dockerignore_

or the filename

_Dockerfile.dockerignore_

If the file `Dockerfile.dockerignore` is a symlink pointing to for instance `../testfile`,
local podman will use `../testfile` 


If I repeat the experiment but use the filename _.dockerignore_ instead of the filename _Dockerfile.dockerignore_, in other words

```
$ rm Dockerfile.dockerignore
$ ln -s ../testfile .dockerignore
```
local podman will not use `../testfile`  

I think restricting local podman would be good but the scope for this PR is the remote case.
How is 'an arbitrary location outside the build context' defined? A symlink that resolves to a path outside the build context?
